# `Golden()`: Bandwidth Selection {#sec-golden}

## Overview

`Golden()` finds the optimal bandwidth $h$ for the geographically weighted model
using a **Golden Section Search (GSS)** algorithm. It evaluates a criterion
function (CV or AICc) at candidate bandwidth values and narrows the search interval
until convergence.

::: {.callout-note}
## Run Golden() before gwzinbr()
Always run `Golden()` first to determine `h`, then pass that value to `gwzinbr(h = ...)`.
Using an arbitrary bandwidth leads to either over-smoothing (global-like estimates)
or under-smoothing (noisy, unstable local estimates).
:::

## Function signature

```r
Golden(
  data,
  formula,
  xvarinf   = NULL,
  weight    = NULL,
  lat,
  long,
  globalmin = TRUE,
  method,
  model     = "zinb",
  bandwidth = "cv",
  offset    = NULL,
  force     = FALSE,
  maxg      = 100,
  distancekm = FALSE
)
```

## Parameter reference

### Data and model specification

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `data` | `data.frame` | â€” | Dataset. Must be a plain data frame (not `sf`). |
| `formula` | `formula` | â€” | Model formula for the count component, as in `lm()`. Example: `cases ~ x1 + x2`. |
| `xvarinf` | `character` | `NULL` | Column name(s) for covariates of the zero-inflation component. Set to `NULL` for non-zero-inflated models. |
| `weight` | `character` | `NULL` | Column name containing sample weights. `NULL` gives equal weights. |
| `offset` | `character` | `NULL` | Column name of the offset (must be on the **log scale**). `NULL` sets offset to zero for all observations. |

### Spatial specification

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `lat` | `character` | â€” | Column name for the Y-coordinate (latitude or northing). |
| `long` | `character` | â€” | Column name for the X-coordinate (longitude or easting). |
| `distancekm` | `logical` | `FALSE` | If `TRUE`, multiply Euclidean distance by 111 to approximate kilometres. Use only with decimal degree coordinates. |

### Bandwidth search settings

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `method` | `character` | â€” | Kernel type: `"adaptive_bsq"` (bi-square adaptive) or `"fixed_g"` (Gaussian fixed). |
| `bandwidth` | `character` | `"cv"` | Optimisation criterion: `"cv"` (cross-validation) or `"aic"` (corrected AIC). |
| `globalmin` | `logical` | `TRUE` | If `TRUE`, divides the search space into three overlapping intervals and runs GSS on each, then returns the global minimum across all three. Strongly recommended. |

### Model settings

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `model` | `character` | `"zinb"` | Distribution family: `"zinb"`, `"zip"`, `"negbin"`, or `"poisson"`. Must match the model you will pass to `gwzinbr()`. |
| `force` | `logical` | `FALSE` | If `FALSE` and zero-inflation is unnecessary (expected zeros â‰Ą observed zeros), the function automatically falls back to the non-zero-inflated version. Set `TRUE` to override. |
| `maxg` | `integer` | `100` | Maximum iterations for the zero-inflation subproblem. Increase if convergence warnings appear. |

## Return value

`Golden()` returns an invisible named list:

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `$h_values` | Initial $h_0, h_1, h_2, h_3$ values for each GSS run |
| `$iterations` | Data frame: all $(h_1, \text{CV}_1, h_2, \text{CV}_2)$ pairs evaluated |
| `$gss_results` | Data frame: optimal $h$ and criterion per GSS interval |
| `$min_bandwidth` | **Scalar**: the final optimal bandwidth to pass to `gwzinbr()` |

## The Golden Section Search algorithm

The GSS is a classical derivative-free optimisation method for unimodal functions.
Starting with an interval $[a, b]$, it evaluates the criterion at two interior
points $h_1 = b - r(b - a)$ and $h_2 = a + r(b - a)$ where $r = 0.618$
(the golden ratio reciprocal). After comparing the criterion values, the worse
half of the interval is discarded and the process repeats.

When `globalmin = TRUE`, the full range is split into three segments and GSS runs
on each. The overall minimum is the best across segments. This guards against
local minima in non-unimodal criterion functions.

## Adaptive vs fixed bandwidth {#sec-method}

### `method = "adaptive_bsq"` (recommended for irregular grids)

$h$ is an **integer**: the number of nearest neighbours included in each local
regression. The physical radius adapts to local point density â€” sparse regions
get a larger radius, dense regions a smaller one.

- Search space: $[5, N]$ (at least 5 neighbours, at most all $N$ observations)
- Output `$min_bandwidth` is rounded to an integer with `floor()`

### `method = "fixed_g"` (appropriate for regular grids)

$h$ is a **distance** in the same units as the coordinates (metres, km, or degrees
depending on your CRS and `distancekm`).

- Search space: $[0, \max(\text{pairwise distances})]$

## CV vs AIC criterion {#sec-criterion}

| Criterion | Formula | Characteristics |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| `"cv"` | $\sum_i w_i(y_i - \hat{y}_{-i})^2$ | Leave-one-out; focuses on predictive accuracy; can favour small $h$ |
| `"aic"` | $\text{AICc} = 2\hat{k} - 2\hat{\ell} + \frac{2\hat{k}(\hat{k}+1)}{N-\hat{k}-1}$ | Penalises effective parameters $\hat{k} = \text{tr}(\mathbf{S})$; balances fit and complexity |

AICc is generally preferred for inference; CV for prediction.

## Examples

### Minimal example (Poisson, adaptive, CV)

```{r}
#| label: golden-minimal
#| eval: false

library(gwzinbr)
data(southkorea_covid19)

gss <- Golden(
  data      = southkorea_covid19,
  formula   = n_covid1 ~ diff_sd,
  lat       = "y", long = "x",
  offset    = "ln_total",
  model     = "poisson",
  method    = "adaptive_bsq",
  bandwidth = "cv",
  globalmin = FALSE,
  distancekm = FALSE
)

gss$min_bandwidth
```

### Recommended: global minimum, AICc, Negative Binomial

```{r}
#| label: golden-nb-aic
#| eval: false

gss_nb <- Golden(
  data      = southkorea_covid19,
  formula   = n_covid1 ~ Morbidity + high_sch_p + Healthcare_access +
                         diff_sd + Crowding + Migration + Health_behavior,
  lat       = "y", long = "x",
  offset    = "ln_total",
  model     = "negbin",
  method    = "adaptive_bsq",
  bandwidth = "aic",
  globalmin = TRUE,
  distancekm = TRUE,
  force     = TRUE
)

# Optimal bandwidth
gss_nb$min_bandwidth

# All iterations (useful for diagnosing convergence)
head(gss_nb$iterations)
```

### Inspecting the convergence table

```{r}
#| label: golden-iterations
#| eval: false

# After running gss_nb above:
gss_nb$iterations
#   GSS_count      h1      aic1      h2      aic2
# 1         1  13.83   892.41   21.29   878.03
# 2         1  21.29   878.03   25.91   873.18
# ...
```

Each row shows one iteration of the GSS. The algorithm has converged when
$|h_3 - h_0| \leq \text{tol} \cdot (|h_1| + |h_2|)$ (default tolerance = 0.1).

## Common issues

**`Golden()` returns a very small bandwidth (close to 5 for adaptive):**
The criterion is monotonically decreasing â€” the model improves by using fewer and
fewer neighbours. This suggests the spatial signal is very local or that the global
model already fits well. Try `globalmin = TRUE` and `bandwidth = "aic"` to add a
complexity penalty.

**Convergence warnings from the zero-inflation part:**
Increase `maxg` or set `force = FALSE` to let the function automatically fall back
to a non-zero-inflated model.

**Very long runtime:**
`Golden()` re-fits the full GWR model at each candidate $h$. For $N > 200$ and
a ZINB model, each evaluation can take minutes. Use `globalmin = FALSE` for a
quick diagnostic run, then `globalmin = TRUE` for the final search.
